pattern: Remembering only the first index where each character appeared turns the search for the widest matching pair into a single linear scan.
hints: You only ever need to know the earliest position a character has shown up at, not every position.
Walk the string once, and the first time you see a repeat of a character you've already recorded, you can immediately measure the gap between the two occurrences.
The answer is the largest such gap (current index minus the remembered first index, minus one), or -1 if no character ever repeats.

edge_inputs.description: statement example two, gap of two
statement example three, no repeats at all
first and last character repeat, spanning the whole string
multiple different repeated characters, must pick the largest gap
character repeats three times, must use the very first occurrence, not the middle one

stress.description: random lowercase strings up to the max length, biased toward a small alphabet so repeats are common
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 300))
    alphabet = 'abcde'
    s = ''.join(rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(n))
    return [s]
